text.title: Evidence Hierarchy and Signal Weighting
text.summary: The 5-level validation evidence hierarchy with explicit weighting rules, commitment signal types, and how to avoid common signal mis-classification errors.

rule.statement: Declare a problem validated only when at least 3 tier-1 or tier-2 signals come from cold, non-network respondents. Any conclusion drawn from fewer or weaker signals is a hypothesis, not a validation.
rule.rationale: Behavioral commitments (payment, sign-up) are costly signals that predict actual adoption. Stated preferences are cheap and optimistic. The 2026 update adds the non-network requirement because warm contacts systematically over-report demand.

list.item: Level 1 (strongest): users paid for a solution — actual purchase behavior.
Level 2: users committed — signed up, provided a letter of intent, invested significant time.
Level 3: users engaged deeply with a prototype — used it, provided feedback, returned.
Level 4: users expressed strong interest — stated intent, agreed to follow-up.
Level 5 (weakest): users said they have the problem — acknowledgment without any action.

rule.statement: Weight tier-1 and tier-2 signals from non-network respondents 5x higher than equivalent signals from warm contacts.
rule.rationale: Network contacts compliment to preserve the relationship. Cold strangers who pay or commit are providing unbiased, costly signals.

list.item: Time commitment: agreed to a follow-up, spent 30+ minutes in a prototype, introduced you to another potential user.
Reputation commitment: introduced you to their boss or team lead; named you in a group message.
Money commitment: paid a pre-order deposit; signed a letter of intent with decision-maker approval.

antipattern.description: Treating "I would definitely introduce you to my team" as a level-2 commitment signal.
antipattern.reason: Future-tense statements are level-4 interest at best. Level-2 commitment requires the introduction to have actually happened (past tense + evidence).

rule.statement: Re-validate problem assumptions every quarter; AI-adjacent markets shift rapidly and validated problems from 6 months ago may no longer hold.
rule.rationale: Validation is not a one-time gate. Market conditions, competitor solutions, and regulation change the problem landscape. A quarterly check prevents building on outdated assumptions.
